title: Green finance or financing green? Bridging the EU’s sustainable-finance and capital-market architectures
gist: Bruegel argues the EU's sustainable-finance disclosure regime (taxonomy, CSRD, SFDR, double materiality) has been leapfrogged by market-led ISSB/ICMA standards, generates costly compliance with little investor uptake, and should be pared back toward global standards and a more market-driven capital-markets agenda. EU taxonomy-aligned green bonds are under 10% of the market while ICMA-standard issuance dominates.

themes.theme: EU green-bond standard is failing to scale

insights.claim: EU taxonomy-aligned green bond issuance made up less than 10 percent of the green bond market as of 2025, despite a decade of EU effort to set the global standard.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: The EU's maximalist green-finance rulebook is not capturing market share; investors are routing around it, so EU-taxonomy-gated products will stay niche and illiquid.
insights.quote: EU taxonomy-aligned green bond issuance made up less than 10 percent of the market as of 2025
insights.locator: 2.4 The EU rulebook has been leapfrogged

insights.claim: The EU's own green bond standard, launched December 2024, produced only 13 transactions in its first nine months, concentrated in utilities and finance issuers like Terna, A2A, Snam, Iberdrola, EDP, EIB and ABN Amro.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: Adoption is thin and issuer-concentrated; watch whether the standard broadens beyond energy/finance reframing before assuming a viable EU-standard market exists.
insights.quote: Responsible Investor found 13 transactions in the first nine months of its use, largely in finance and energy
insights.locator: 3.2 Green bonds as a testing ground

insights.claim: The ICMA Green Bond Standard covers roughly $3 trillion in market capitalisation with about $700 billion in annual issuance, dwarfing the EU standard.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: ICMA is the de facto market benchmark; any green-bond exposure or index construction should anchor to ICMA, not the EU taxonomy.
insights.quote: the International Capital Markets Association’s (ICMA) Green Bond Standards (section 2.4) now cover about $3 trillion in market capitalisation, with annual issuance of $700 billion per year
insights.locator: 3.2 Green bonds as a testing ground

insights.claim: The EU itself issued more than €80 billion in NextGenerationEU green bonds using the ICMA principles rather than its own EU green bond standard, with no announced plan to switch.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: Even the EU's own flagship issuer rejects the EU standard in practice — a strong tell that the standard lacks market function.
insights.quote: the EU has issued more than €80 billion in green bonds as of April 2026
insights.locator: 3.2 Green bonds as a testing ground

insights.claim: Only 16 percent of EU companies’ eligible capital expenditure is taxonomy-aligned, with large gaps in transitioning sectors such as transport, construction, infrastructure, real estate and chemicals.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: Taxonomy-aligned portfolios are structurally undiversified and exclude the very sectors that most need transition capital — a concentration risk baked into the rules.
insights.quote: Only 16 percent of EU companies’ eligible capital expenditure is taxonomy-aligned
insights.locator: 3.2 Green bonds as a testing ground

insights.claim: In a 2023 EU consultation, 84 percent of respondents did not believe SFDR-required disclosures were significantly useful to investors and 83 percent said they were used as a marketing tool rather than a disclosure framework.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: The flagship disclosure regulation is widely seen as greenwashing infrastructure, not decision-useful data — supporting a thesis that the regime gets dismantled or marginalised.
insights.quote: 84 percent of respondents (249 out of 296) did not believe required disclosures were “significantly useful” to investors, and 83 percent (245 out of 296) said disclosures were being used as a marketing tool

insights.claim: EFRAG identified roughly €4 billion in savings from simplifying the ESRS while being able to assess benefits to data users only in qualitative terms, implying the disclosure regime's costs are concrete and its benefits unmeasurable.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: A cost-benefit case where costs are quantified and benefits are not is a classic setup for deregulation; expect continued rollback of reporting scope.
insights.quote: readily able to identify around €4 billion in savings from a proposal to simplify the European Sustainability Reporting Standards

insights.claim: Policymakers cannot compel investment or capital-market development merely by forcing more information into the open; treating disclosure as a financial incentive is a category error in EU green-finance design.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: Frames why ESG disclosure mandates won't move capital absent real return signals — a durable lens for judging future EU rules.
insights.quote: Policymakers cannot compel investment or capital-market development solely by forcing more information to become available.

insights.claim: When mandatory disclosure does change company behaviour to cut industrial emissions, it can do so at the cost of lower profitability, and some investors will actively avoid that correlation.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: Implies a structural tension: the disclosure regime can repel the capital it intends to attract, a contrarian read on ESG-as-alpha.
insights.quote: it may do so at a cost of lower profitability (Chen et al, 2018). Some investors may actively avoid such correlations.

themes.theme: Double materiality vs the global standard

insights.claim: The core EU/ISSB divergence is materiality: ISSB uses single materiality (climate factors affecting a company's financials) while the EU mandates double materiality (a firm's impact on the climate overall), and there is no passporting between the two regimes.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: Dual non-compatible regimes mean multinational issuers keep two sets of books; reform toward ISSB compatibility would cut compliance cost and is the author's headline recommendation.
insights.quote: the EU approach calls for ‘double materiality’, which forces companies to assess how their activities affect the climate overall, not just in terms of their own bottom line
insights.locator: 2.4 The EU rulebook has been leapfrogged

insights.claim: ISSB Chair Sue Lloyd said EU rules ask for information that investors would not require and that ESRS contains Green-Deal-driven, policy-focused elements while ISSB standards are intentionally policy-neutral, making reconciliation difficult.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: The global standard-setter publicly rules out easy alignment, so the compliance-cost gap for EU issuers is unlikely to close soon.
insights.quote: the EU rules ask for information that “investors would not require”

insights.claim: The recommended fix is to revamp double materiality into a simplified 'bolt-on' to ISSB rather than a separate full calculation, splitting behaviour-change disclosures from financially material ones.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: Signals the likely reform direction; firms positioned for ISSB-first reporting would benefit if the EU moves this way.
insights.quote: Double materiality should be revamped so that it can act as a ‘bolt-on’ to ISSB, rather than requiring two full sets of calculations.

themes.theme: The Omnibus simplification that didn't simplify

insights.claim: The 2025 Omnibus I package cut CSRD scope by raising thresholds to 1,000 employees or €450 million revenue (from 250/€50m), removing about 90 percent of covered firms rather than reducing rule complexity.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: Scope-cutting instead of complexity-cutting leaves remaining large issuers with the same burden — watch for further rounds of reform.
insights.quote: CSRD reporting thresholds were raised to companies with 1,000 employees or €450 million in revenues, compared to 250 employees and €50 million previously – a 90 percent reduction in firms covered.

insights.claim: CSDDD due-diligence rules were narrowed to firms with 5,000+ employees and €1.5 billion revenue, stripped of mandatory transition plans, and delayed to 2028, raising the prospect of further dilution.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: Supply-chain ESG obligations are being walked back and delayed; compliance-tech and ESG-data vendors face a shrinking mandated market.
insights.quote: CSDDD will now not come into force until 2028, a year later than the previous deadline

insights.claim: EFRAG proposed cutting the number of ESRS data points by about 70 percent, signalling a sharp retreat from granular sustainability reporting.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: A 70% data-point cut materially shrinks the ESG-reporting compliance market and the data available for ESG screening.
insights.quote: EFRAG, the Commission-backed advisory group that sets reporting standards, proposed reducing the number of data points by about 70 percent

themes.theme: Political retreat and the ESG rebrand

insights.claim: Under Trump, the US has withdrawn from international climate initiatives and discouraged ESG funds, reinforcing a divergence where European asset managers stay active in sustainable investing while US peers increasingly vote against social and environmental resolutions.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: A widening transatlantic ESG split shapes flows and proxy-voting; positions sensitive to US vs EU asset-manager behaviour should price this divergence.
insights.quote: European asset managers remaining active in sustainable investing, while their US counterparts increasingly vote against social and environmental resolutions
insights.locator: 2.1 Sustainable finance disclosures in theory and practice

insights.claim: The sustainability-investment brand keeps shifting under political pressure — ESG gave way to CSR and then to more anodyne terms like 'resilience' and 'security' — indicating the label, not the underlying activity, is what is being managed.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: Rebranding signals reputational risk management; 'resilience'/'security' framing is where the next wave of mandates and capital may be marketed.
insights.quote: The once popular ESG label has given way to CSR, which itself has been replaced by more anodyne terms such as ‘resilience’ and ‘security’

insights.claim: EU Financial Services Commissioner Maria Luis Albuquerque said she is 'agnostic' about green finance, treating it as part of the Clean Industrial Deal and insisting sustainable finance must offer competitive returns rather than being forced.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: The official owning capital-markets policy has effectively sidelined green finance from the core markets agenda — a top-down signal of deprioritisation.
insights.quote: Sustainable finance, in order to make its way to capital markets, has to be a good business case. We will not force private money into anything.

insights.claim: The EU may need to explicitly classify defence and security spending as sustainable to attract private capital to its strategic buildup, after firms imposed defence-investment restrictions for compliance and reputational reasons.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: A move to bring defence under the sustainability umbrella would unlock ESG-constrained capital for European defence names — a watch item for the sector.
insights.quote: the European Commission may need to explicitly tie security to its sustainability goals if it wants to attract private capital to the EU’s strategic buildup

themes.theme: Capital-markets union plumbing and reform recipe

insights.claim: A decade after it was proposed, the EU capital markets union remains deadlocked and fragmentation is cited (Draghi, Letta) as a top barrier to growth and competitiveness, prompting a rebrand to the 'Savings and Investments Union'.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: Persistent CMU deadlock keeps European equity/VC markets shallow relative to the US — a structural drag on EU asset valuations and financing.
insights.quote: capital markets deadlock persists, with capital market fragmentation considered a top barrier to economic growth and competitiveness
insights.locator: 2.3 The EU and the rest of the world

insights.claim: Europe's biggest financial-integration wins came from low-profile technical files — the diversified/unified EU debt funding strategy creating one trading market for EU debt, and the move to T+1 settlement — not from headline political projects.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: Suggests the actionable EU progress to watch is plumbing (settlement, unified debt), not political ESG mandates — where real liquidity benefits accrue.
insights.quote: the move to T+1 settlement, in which the EU and the United Kingdom are working to simultaneously shorten trade settlement timelines by one day

insights.claim: The author recommends limiting securitisation-linked government guarantees to green and SME loans with first-loss structures, and explicitly rejects France Trésor's call for guarantees on mortgage-backed securities as importing the worst of the US model.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: Flags where EU securitisation policy may land — SME/green first-loss guarantees rather than MBS — relevant for European structured-credit markets.
insights.quote: it was wrong on guarantees, recommending government support for mortgage-backed securities, which would import the worst part of the US securitisation landscape
insights.locator: 4.4 The role of subsidies and guarantees

insights.claim: The EU's sustainable-finance framework is debt/bond-centric and not instrument-neutral, reinforcing Europe's chronic over-reliance on debt over equity; the fix is to bring equities and venture capital into sustainable-finance planning.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: A pivot toward green equity/VC frameworks would shift where sustainable capital flows; today's bond bias is a structural limit on EU green-equity products.
insights.quote: the framework is not instrument-neutral (Merler, 2025) and also reinforces the EU’s longstanding dependence on debt compared to equity

themes.theme: Standards fragmentation and the failed Brussels effect

insights.claim: More than 50 green taxonomies are in use or development globally, so the EU's bet that the 'Brussels effect' would make its taxonomy the world standard has not materialised.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: A fragmented global taxonomy landscape means cross-border issuers face permanent multi-rulebook costs; no single regime captures the market.
insights.quote: More than 50 green taxonomies are in use or development globally
insights.locator: 2.4 The EU rulebook has been leapfrogged

insights.claim: Market standards have proven more consistent than government signals: the US SEC dropped its ISSB-related climate disclosure rules in 2025 yet US financial firms continued to use ISSB standards voluntarily.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: Argues voluntary market-led standards outlast political reversals — a case for anchoring to ISSB/ICMA over politically driven regimes.
insights.quote: the US, represented by the Securities and Exchange Commission, dropped its ISSB-related rules in 2025, but US financial firms continue to use the ISSB standards
insights.locator: 2.4 The EU rulebook has been leapfrogged

insights.claim: The EU was judged 'materially non-compliant' with a main pillar of the Basel bank capital standards in 2014 and has not resolved it, undercutting its claim to multilateral-standards leadership.
insights.speaker: Bruegel
insights.so_what: Track record of departing from global standards weakens confidence the EU will converge to ISSB voluntarily.
insights.quote: the EU in 2014 was judged, ‘materially non-compliant’ with one of the main pillars of the Basel capital standards for banks
insights.locator: 2.4 The EU rulebook has been leapfrogged
